Threat.value: Having strong physical reactions when something reminded you of the stressful experience
Suddenly feeling or acting as if the stressful experience were actually happening again

Memory.value: My memories of the frightening event are mostly pictures or images.
I can't seem to put the frightening event into words.
When I remember the frightening event I feel like it is happening right now.

Negative Appraisals.value: My reactions since the event mean that I am going crazy
Nothing good can happen to me anymore
I can't trust that I will do the right thing

Maladaptive Strategies.value: Being reminded of the event by others
try hard not think about the trauma
keep mind occupied all the time

issues.item: Hypervigilance towards people: vigilant for dangerous people
issues.explanation: With a bereavement trauma, hypervigilance is more plausibly directed toward reminders of the deceased, places, anniversaries, or situations linked to the loss rather than specifically toward dangerous people. This item introduces a threat focus that does not fit the rest of the profile.

issues.item: Other-Blame Rumination: I dwell on what other people have done to me
issues.explanation: This is somewhat inconsistent with bereavement as the primary trauma, because the other selected items mainly reflect loss-related re-experiencing and self-focused negative appraisals rather than interpersonal victimization. It could fit if the death involved blameable others, but that link is not supported elsewhere in the profile.

vignette:
The patient is a 58-year-old divorced North American man presenting with persistent posttraumatic stress symptoms following the bereavement of a close loved one. His PCL-5 score of 47 is consistent with clinically significant distress. He describes a pattern of living on edge, with physical touch, certain related smells, and moments of being seen by others acting as potent reminders of the loss. In those moments he becomes acutely activated, reporting a jumpy startle response, strong bodily reactions, and brief episodes in which he suddenly feels and acts as though the event is happening again.

His recollections of the bereavement are fragmentary and image-based. He says the memories arrive as pictures rather than a coherent story, and that he cannot easily put the experience into words. When he remembers the event, it feels immediate and present rather than in the past. These memory intrusions are a central part of his daily suffering, repeatedly pulling him back into the emotional and bodily state associated with the loss.

He has developed harsh beliefs about what his reactions mean. He worries that the intensity of his symptoms means he is going crazy, feels that nothing good can happen to him anymore, and doubts that he can trust himself to do the right thing. These appraisals sit at the centre of his presentation, shaping how he interprets his experience and how he tries to manage it. He spends much of the day trying not to think about the trauma, keeping his mind occupied as much as possible, and avoiding reminders when others bring up the event.

Despite these efforts, the symptoms remain self-perpetuating. When he is physically activated, the intrusive memories become more vivid and immediate, and the memories in turn intensify his threat state. At the same time, the appraisals continue to colour both his bodily reactions and the form his memories take, leaving him caught in a cycle of fear, vigilance, and mental strain. The overall picture is one of narrowing daily life, strained confidence, and emotional isolation, with reminders of the loss continuing to dominate his sense of self and safety.
